executive_summary: Zerg AI has a genuinely differentiated positioning — hardware-in-the-loop validation, continuous operating loops, and real-signal correctness for mission-critical systems — that no competitor owns. However, the landing page fails to convert on that differentiation: zero social proof, a high-friction primary CTA ('Talk to us'), and a subheadline requiring multiple careful readings to parse leave Zerg looking nascent next to competitors like Devin and Cursor who lead with named enterprise logos, quantified outcomes, and trusted executive testimonials. The technical substance is there; the page architecture and proof infrastructure are not.

patterns_across_competitors: Dark background hero sections — universal across Cursor, Windsurf, Aider, Factory, Anthropic, Sourcegraph, GitHub Copilot, and Devin
Dual CTAs with a clear primary (self-serve/download) and secondary (contact sales/docs) — present on Cursor, Anthropic, Devin, Factory, Windsurf, and GitHub Copilot

Named enterprise customer logos in the hero or directly below — Devin (Nubank, Stripe, Airtable), GitHub (Shopify, Stripe, GM, Coca-Cola), Replit (Databricks, Zillow, Gusto), Factory (Podium, Groq, Chainguard), Windsurf (Reply), Sourcegraph (Stripe)

Quantified productivity/outcome metrics displayed prominently — Cursor ('40,000 NVIDIA engineers'), GitHub ('55% more productive'), Devin ('8x efficiency, 20x cost savings'), Windsurf ('94% of code written by AI'), Sourcegraph ('30% cost/task reduction')
Named executive testimonials with title and company attribution — Cursor (Jensen Huang/NVIDIA, Patrick Collison/Stripe, Greg Brockman/OpenAI), Windsurf (Garry Tan/YC), Replit (Ali Ghodsi/Databricks)
A 'how it works' or product feature showcase section with visual treatment — Factory (tabbed 01–05 workflow), Replit (Parallel Agents + Infinite Canvas), Windsurf (Cascade + Devin + Tab breakdown)
Explicit docs or getting-started navigation item — Aider, Anthropic, Factory, GitHub Copilot all surface documentation as a trust and activation signal
Funding or analyst credibility signals for enterprise trust — Factory ($150M Series C, $1.5B valuation, Sequoia/Khosla), Sourcegraph (SOC2 Type II + ISO27001), GitHub Copilot (Trust Center link)

best_in_class.element: Enterprise social proof with named outcomes
best_in_class.description: Devin leads with a detailed Nubank case study featuring Jose Carlos Castro by name and title, with verified quantitative outcomes: '8x engineering time efficiency gain', '20x cost savings', '6 million lines of code migrated', '40 minutes per sub-task dropped to 10 after fine-tuning'. This is more credible than generic logo stacks because it names the task, the person, and the measurable result — exactly the format enterprise buyers need to build an internal business case.

Cursor has testimonials from Jensen Huang ('every one of our engineers, some 40,000, are now assisted by AI'), Patrick Collison ('quickly grew from hundreds to thousands of extremely enthusiastic Stripe employees'), Greg Brockman, and Andrej Karpathy. Each is attributed with full name, title, and company. The social proof stack communicates 'this is what the most credible technical leaders in the world are using' without Cursor making that claim directly.

Factory's hero explicitly claims 'The only software development agents that work everywhere you do' and positions around 'without changing your tools, models, or workflow' — this directly counters the objection that adopting a new AI tool disrupts existing engineering culture. They also use a CLI install command as the primary CTA ('curl -fsSL https://app.factory.ai/cli | sh'), which is a trust signal for engineers and reduces signup friction to zero.

Devin frames cost as '20x cheaper than human engineer hourly rates' and 'completing projects months ahead of schedule' — this is ROI language that makes enterprise budget conversations easy. No Zerg competitor uses actual price points on the hero; they all anchor to the cost of NOT having the tool.

market_gaps: Hardware-in-the-loop validation: No competitor — not Cursor, not Devin, not Factory — explicitly targets engineering teams whose correctness requirements involve physical devices, simulators, or real production signals. Zerg uniquely owns this and no competitor is building toward it.

The 'AI that doesn't stop at code generation' positioning: Every competitor either positions as 'autocomplete+' (Cursor, Copilot) or 'app builder' (Bolt, Replit, Lovable). Zerg's Map→Build→Prove→Run loop — especially the Prove and Run phases — is uncontested positioning if surfaced aggressively on the page.

Mission-critical domain specialization: Aider targets terminal users, Bolt/Replit/Lovable target non-coders, Cursor/Windsurf target general engineers. No competitor explicitly names hardware, simulations, control planes, COBOL/legacy migrations, or CVE remediation as their core domain. Zerg's subheadline lists all of these but buries them.

Continuous post-delivery monitoring and drift correction: Devin and Factory focus on task completion. Zerg's 'Run' phase — autonomous monitoring for API drift, dependency changes, and requirement changes with autonomous follow-on fixes — has no clear competitor analogue and is a major enterprise retention/stickiness story.

Connector-first architecture for vendor-integrated systems: Sourcegraph owns code intelligence; no one owns the problem of AI that understands third-party APIs, hardware protocols, and legacy vendor integrations as first-class inputs to code generation.

strengths: The tagline 'AI systems for mission-critical work' is clean, confident, and differentiating — it signals premium without being vague in the way 'AI pair programming' (Aider) or 'What will you build today?' (Bolt) are

The Map→Build→Prove→Run loop is a genuinely differentiated framework that no competitor has — it positions Zerg as a system rather than a tool, which is exactly the right framing for enterprise buyers
The explicit rejection of 'another chat box' and 'we do not stop at prompt-to-code' is strong competitive positioning that Devin and Factory only gesture toward

Specific, credible metrics (24-day JS-to-TS migration, 2-3 production integrations/week, 24/7 continuous runtime) are more operationally specific than most competitor claims — they feel like real customer outcomes, not marketing assertions
The product surface area (ZDE + ZTC + Zergboard + Zerg Cloud) signals platform depth rather than single-tool risk, which matters for enterprise procurement
gaps: Zero customer logos, zero testimonials, zero press mentions — every competitor from Aider (Reddit/HN quotes) to Cursor (Fortune 500 CEOs) has social proof infrastructure; Zerg has none, which triggers enterprise buyer risk aversion

Primary CTA is 'Talk to us' — the highest-friction entry point possible; Cursor, Aider, Factory, Windsurf all offer immediate self-serve access; even Devin and Cognition (enterprise-first) have 'Try Devin' CTAs before 'Contact Sales'

No visible trust or compliance signals — SOC2, security page, data retention policy — while Sourcegraph (SOC2 + ISO27001), GitHub Copilot (Trust Center), Devin (named enterprise tier), and Replit (SOC2 + SSO/SAML) all prominently surface these for enterprise buyers
No visual demos, screenshots, or video — Aider has an embedded video in the hero, Cursor shows IDE screenshots, Factory has a tabbed product showcase; Zerg is all prose

The subheadline ('Zerg works with teams whose products depend on hard technical systems: hardware, simulations, data and control planes, vendor integrations, migrations, and production workflows') requires deliberate reading to parse — it's a list, not a claim

No pricing signal or self-serve entry point — even contact-sales-first competitors like Devin and Factory offer a free tier or self-serve CLI to capture demand before the sales conversation; Zerg has a waitlist with no trial path

No 'how it works' visual — the Map/Build/Prove/Run steps are described in prose but no competitor relies on prose alone for product education; Factory uses numbered tabs, Replit uses a workflow diagram, Devin uses metric-anchored feature blocks
Documentation is not surfaced in navigation — Aider, Anthropic, Factory, GitHub all make docs a nav-level item, which signals product maturity and lets technical evaluators self-qualify
zerg_assessment.positioning_clarity: Zerg's positioning is intellectually clear to someone who reads the full subheadline carefully, but it does not communicate fast. The value proposition — 'we build and continuously operate software for systems where correctness requires real signals, not just tests' — requires 3-4 sentences to extract from the current page. Competitors communicate their core claim in 6 words (Factory: 'Agent-Native Software Development') or 8 words (Cursor: 'The best way to code with AI'). Zerg's tagline ('AI systems for mission-critical work') is strong but the subheadline does not accelerate understanding — it expands the problem surface area in a way that diffuses focus rather than sharpening it.
conversion_bottlenecks: 'Talk to us' as primary CTA — enterprise buyers at exploratory stage will not convert on this; they need a self-serve signal (demo, trial, interactive product) before they will schedule a sales call

No social proof above the fold — buyers arriving from outbound or paid channels have no immediate anchor of 'companies like mine use this'; the absence of logos triggers the first objection ('are they real?') before the visitor reads the value prop

Subheadline length — the current subheadline is 52 words; Cursor's is 12, Windsurf's is 9, Factory's is 30 with a clear benefit per clause; a 52-word subheadline increases cognitive load at exactly the moment when the visitor is deciding whether to keep reading

No proof section for the 24-day migration claim — this is Zerg's Nubank moment (Devin's best conversion asset) but it is presented as a bare metric, not a story with a company name, a problem statement, and a reviewable outcome

Waitlist/contact-only model with no visible self-serve path — buyers who want to evaluate technically before engaging sales cannot do so, and Zerg Cloud is listed as a secondary CTA without any description of what it is or what the entry point looks like

recommendations.title: Add a named enterprise case study with quantified outcomes above the fold
recommendations.current_state: Zerg has three bare metrics (24 days, 2-3/week, 24/7) with no company name, no human name, no problem context, and no story
recommendations.recommendation: Develop one full named case study in the format Devin uses for Nubank: company name + named spokesperson + title + specific problem + specific outcome metrics + a quote. Place it directly below the hero, not in a buried proof section. If a customer cannot be named publicly yet, use anonymized industry + metric ('A Fortune 500 infrastructure team migrated 6M lines of legacy Java in 24 days with reviewable checkpoints') — still more credible than bare numbers.
recommendations.rationale: Devin's Nubank case study ('8x engineering time efficiency gain, 20x cost savings, 6M lines migrated, Jose Carlos Castro, Senior PM, Nubank') is the single highest-credibility element on their page. Enterprise buyers constructing an internal business case need a reference point — ideally a named peer. Zerg's existing metrics are operationally specific enough to be credible; they just lack the attribution that triggers trust.

recommendations.title: Replace 'Talk to us' primary CTA with a low-friction self-serve entry point
recommendations.current_state: 'Talk to us' is the primary hero CTA — the highest-friction conversion action possible for a technical product
recommendations.recommendation: Make 'Try Zerg Cloud' the primary CTA with a secondary 'Talk to us / Contact sales'. If Zerg Cloud has a working trial or sandbox, surface that. If not, implement a Calendly-style 'Book a 20-min demo' that pre-qualifies and schedules rather than asking for a cold conversation. Alternatively, follow Factory's model and expose a CLI install command as the hero CTA ('curl -fsSL install.zergai.com | sh') — this is a zero-friction, high-trust signal for the target engineering audience.
recommendations.rationale: Every competitor except Devin (enterprise-first, $500M+ valuations) leads with a self-serve or low-friction entry. Factory uses a CLI install command. Aider uses 'Get Started'. Windsurf uses 'Download for macOS'. Even Cognition uses 'Try Devin' before 'Contact Sales'. 'Talk to us' positions Zerg as a pre-product consultancy, not a platform — and repels the technical evaluators who would become internal champions.

recommendations.title: Rewrite the subheadline to lead with the claim, not the problem surface
recommendations.current_state: 'Zerg works with teams whose products depend on hard technical systems: hardware, simulations, data and control planes, vendor integrations, migrations, and production workflows. We model the environment, build the software, validate against real signals, and keep it operating as the system changes.' — 52 words, list-forward
recommendations.recommendation: Reduce to a single sentence that leads with the differentiated claim and ends with the audience: 'Zerg generates, validates, and continuously operates software for systems where correctness requires real signals — not just tests.' Then add a second sentence that names the domains: 'Built for teams running hardware integrations, legacy migrations, data pipelines, and production control systems.' This is ~30 words total, front-loads the differentiator (real signal validation), and names the target audience without requiring the reader to infer it from a list.
recommendations.rationale: Cursor's subheadline ('Built to make you extraordinarily productive, Cursor is the best way to code with AI') is 15 words and communicates a single claim. Factory's ('The only software development agents that work everywhere you do') is 14 words with a superlative anchor. At 52 words with a comma-separated list structure, Zerg's subheadline fails at its primary job: sustaining scroll momentum from the headline.

recommendations.title: Add a logo bar with customer or integration logos
recommendations.current_state: No logos on the page — not customers, not integration partners, not press
recommendations.recommendation: Add a 'Trusted by' or 'Works with' logo bar directly below the hero CTA section. If no named customers are available yet, use integration/technology logos: AWS, GitHub, PostgreSQL, Datadog, Slack, and any hardware/industrial partners. Factory does this with Khosla/Sequoia/Blackstone funding logos when customer logos aren't dominant. A technology logo bar signals ecosystem depth and reduces the 'are they real?' objection for enterprise buyers.
recommendations.rationale: Of the 13 competitors analyzed, 10 have visible logo bars or named social proof in the hero area. The only exception is Aider (which compensates with 44K GitHub stars and 6.8M installs). The absence of logos is the fastest trust-disqualifier in B2B SaaS — enterprise buyers pattern-match on 'do companies like mine use this' before they read the value prop.

recommendations.title: Add a visual 'How it works' section for the Map→Build→Prove→Run loop
recommendations.current_state: The four-phase loop is described in prose feature bullets — no visual hierarchy, no diagram, no numbered flow
recommendations.recommendation: Build a numbered four-step horizontal or vertical flow section: [1] Map — model your environment [2] Build — generate to spec [3] Prove — validate against live signals [4] Run — monitor and self-correct. Each step gets a 1-sentence description and an icon or micro-diagram. This is exactly the format Factory uses for their 01–05 product flow and Replit uses for their Parallel Agents section. Make 'Prove' and 'Run' visually prominent — they are the most differentiated phases and currently receive equal visual weight to 'Map' and 'Build'.
recommendations.rationale: The Map→Build→Prove→Run framework is Zerg's core differentiator against every competitor. But in prose format it reads like a feature list. Devin's 'fleet of agents', Replit's 'Parallel Agents', and Factory's tabbed workflow sections all use visual treatment to make the product model memorable. A visitor who leaves Zerg's page should be able to recall the four phases — that requires visual encoding, not text scanning.

recommendations.title: Surface a direct competitive comparison statement against 'AI code assistants'
recommendations.current_state: Zerg says 'we do not stop at prompt-to-code' but this is in body copy — the contrast with Cursor/Copilot/Aider is implicit, not explicit
recommendations.recommendation: Add a one-section explicit differentiation block: 'Most AI coding tools stop when the code is written. Zerg doesn't.' Follow with a two-column or three-row comparison: [Other tools] Generates code on request | [Zerg] Validates against live systems. [Other tools] Works in your IDE | [Zerg] Runs 24/7 in production. [Other tools] You own correctness | [Zerg] We monitor and fix drift autonomously. This is the format Windsurf uses to contrast with Cursor and Factory uses to contrast with autocomplete tools.
recommendations.rationale: Every enterprise buyer evaluating Zerg has already tried or is currently using Cursor, Copilot, or Devin. The unsaid objection is 'how is this different from what I already have?' Zerg's positioning — continuous operation + real signal validation — directly answers this, but only if stated explicitly. Anthropic's 'Built for hackers' differentiates from GUI-first tools in a single phrase; Zerg needs the equivalent clarity.

recommendations.title: Add a security and compliance section or footer trust signal
recommendations.current_state: No SOC2, no data retention policy, no security page, no compliance mention anywhere on the page
recommendations.recommendation: Add a footer trust band with: 'SOC 2 Type II Certified | Zero data retention | Enterprise SSO | Private deployment available' — even as aspirational copy that links to a security page. If SOC2 is in progress, say 'SOC 2 audit in progress'. At minimum, create a /security page linked from the footer and nav, and include 1-2 trust signals in the hero (Sourcegraph's 'Zero data retention' claim and GitHub's 'Trust Center' link are both conversion-positive for enterprise).
recommendations.rationale: Sourcegraph, GitHub Copilot, Replit, and Windsurf all make compliance/security a visible signal. For mission-critical engineering teams — Zerg's stated target — security and data handling are procurement blockers, not nice-to-haves. The absence of any compliance signal on a page targeting 'mission-critical' systems creates cognitive dissonance that will stop enterprise procurement conversations before they start.

recommendations.title: Add a metrics bar to the hero with Zerg's existing operational numbers
recommendations.current_state: Metrics (2-3/week integrations, 24 days for migration, 24/7 runtime) are buried in body copy with no visual prominence
recommendations.recommendation: Add a stats bar directly below or above the hero CTA buttons — 3-4 stats in the format Aider uses ('44K GitHub Stars | 6.8M Installs | 15B Tokens/week'). For Zerg: '2–3 production integrations shipped per week | 24-day large-scale migration | 24/7 autonomous runtime | Real signal validation'. These are already on the page; they need to be elevated to the social proof position where buyers scan for credibility signals before investing attention in the copy.
recommendations.rationale: Aider converts with community metrics because the numbers are prominent and specific. Devin converts with outcome metrics because they are attributed. Zerg has outcome metrics that are more operationally credible than Aider's community numbers — '24 days for a large JS-to-TypeScript migration' is a falsifiable, enterprise-relevant claim. The problem is placement: metrics in body copy are not processed as trust signals; metrics in a hero stat bar are.

recommendations.title: Add use case segmentation section for different buyer personas
recommendations.current_state: Zerg lists use case types in the subheadline (hardware, simulations, migrations, etc.) but has no dedicated use case section with buyer-type framing
recommendations.recommendation: Add a 'Built for teams running...' section with 4-6 named use cases as cards or tabs: [Legacy Migrations] [Hardware Integrations] [CVE Remediation] [Data Pipeline Operations] [Vendor API Platforms] [Production Control Systems]. Each card links to a use case page or expands with a 2-sentence description and a metric. Factory does this with their tabbed feature flow. Bolt does it with role-based cards (PM, Entrepreneur, Marketer). Devin does it with fleet-based use case descriptions.
recommendations.rationale: Zerg's target market is highly heterogeneous — a hardware startup and a bank running COBOL migrations are both in scope. Use case segmentation lets each buyer quickly self-identify and read the content most relevant to their situation. It also provides internal SEO surface area and makes sales conversations easier when the prospect has already read the relevant use case.

recommendations.title: Embed a product demo or screen recording in the hero
recommendations.current_state: No visual product demo anywhere on the page — entirely text and prose
recommendations.recommendation: Add a short (60–90 second) screen recording or animated gif in the hero showing: the Zerg interface receiving a task, the Prove phase running against a live service, and the Run phase detecting drift and creating a follow-on fix. Aider's embedded video demo is their most effective trust signal because it answers 'does this actually work?' before the visitor reads any copy. Even a static UI screenshot showing the Zergboard + ZDE interface is materially better than no visual.
recommendations.rationale: Among the 13 competitors analyzed, only Zerg has no visual product representation on its landing page. Cursor has IDE screenshots. Windsurf has IDE + agent console screenshots. Factory has a CLI demo. Aider has a video. For a product claiming to operate mission-critical software autonomously, the absence of any visible product is the strongest possible trust deficit — it suggests either early-stage or deliberately hidden, neither of which helps enterprise conversion.

quick_wins: Promote the 24-day migration and 2-3/week integrations metrics into a hero stats bar — they are already on the page, just in the wrong position; this is a copy-paste operation with high social proof impact
Change primary CTA from 'Talk to us' to 'Book a 20-min demo' or 'Try Zerg Cloud' — one word/link change that reduces friction for the exploratory buyer segment and doesn't require any product changes
Add a 'Works with' technology logo bar using integration logos (AWS, GitHub, PostgreSQL, Datadog, Slack) — no customer approval needed, signals ecosystem maturity, takes 2 hours to design

Rewrite the subheadline from 52 words to 15-20 words leading with 'validates against real signals' — the core differentiator is buried at the end of the current subheadline; moving it to position 1 costs nothing

Add a security/compliance footer band with 3-4 trust signals (SOC2 status, data retention policy, private deployment option) — mission-critical buyers are disqualifying on compliance before they reach the features section

copy_rewrites.suggested: The AI engineering platform for systems that can't be wrong.
copy_rewrites.why: Current tagline is good but passive — 'for mission-critical work' describes a domain, not a claim. 'Systems that can't be wrong' is active, stakes-raising, and directly names the buyer's actual fear (correctness failures in production). It creates immediate contrast with 'AI coding tools' that trade correctness for speed.

copy_rewrites.current: Zerg works with teams whose products depend on hard technical systems: hardware, simulations, data and control planes, vendor integrations, migrations, and production workflows. We model the environment, build the software, validate against real signals, and keep it operating as the system changes.
copy_rewrites.suggested: Zerg generates, validates against live signals, and continuously operates software for complex technical systems — hardware integrations, legacy migrations, data pipelines, and production control planes. We don't stop when the code ships.
copy_rewrites.why: Lead with the differentiated capability (real signal validation + continuous operation) before naming the domains. End with the direct competitor contrast ('We don't stop when the code ships') that Factory and Devin gesture at but don't say plainly. Reduces from 52 to 35 words while preserving all domain specificity.

'Talk to us' is ambiguous, informal, and high-commitment. 'Book a 20-min demo' is specific about the time investment, implies a structured conversation with an outcome, and signals confidence. It converts exploratory buyers who won't fill out a contact form but will put 20 minutes on a calendar.

Adding a directional arrow and 'Start with' communicates forward momentum and that there is an onboarding experience waiting — not just a landing page. Small change, meaningfully higher click intent signal.

copy_rewrites.current: Validates against live services, simulators, devices, and production signals beyond unit tests
copy_rewrites.suggested: Validates correctness against live systems — not mocks, not unit tests, not synthetic data. Real signals from real environments.
copy_rewrites.why: The current copy lists validation surfaces. The rewrite names what other tools do (mocks, unit tests, synthetic data) and explicitly rejects them — this is the 'Built for hackers' move Anthropic makes. It sharpens differentiation by defining the category Zerg is not in, not just the category it is in.

copy_rewrites.current: Monitors for drift in requirements, APIs, or dependencies and creates follow-on fixes autonomously
copy_rewrites.suggested: Watches for drift in APIs, requirements, and dependencies — and ships the fix without waiting to be asked.
copy_rewrites.why: 'Without waiting to be asked' is the emotional payoff of the Run phase and the clearest contrast with every competitor who waits for a human prompt. The current copy is technically accurate; the rewrite makes the implication explicit and memorable.

copy_rewrites.element: Hero competitive contrast (new copy, not current)
copy_rewrites.current: N/A — no explicit competitive contrast exists on page
copy_rewrites.suggested: Most AI coding tools stop when the code ships. Zerg doesn't. We validate against your live systems and keep the software running as the world around it changes.
copy_rewrites.why: Every enterprise buyer has used or evaluated Cursor, Copilot, or Devin. This copy acknowledges that context and positions Zerg's Run+Prove phases as the answer to the limitation buyers have already experienced. Factory uses 'without changing your tools'; Anthropic uses 'Built for hackers'; Zerg needs the equivalent implicit contrast statement.

new_sections_to_add.section: Named case study / proof of work
new_sections_to_add.rationale: Devin's Nubank case study with Jose Carlos Castro, 8x efficiency, 20x cost savings, and 6M migrated lines is their single highest-conversion page element. Zerg has the raw material (24-day migration, 2-3/week integrations) but no attribution, no story, and no human name. Every enterprise competitor has at least one named customer outcome; Zerg has zero.
new_sections_to_add.content_guidance: Format: Company name (or anonymized '[Fortune 500 infrastructure team]') → Problem (e.g., '6M lines of legacy JavaScript to TypeScript, no dedicated migration team') → Zerg approach (Map phase: 3 days to model codebase. Build + Prove: 21 days of validated, reviewable checkpoints.) → Outcome: metrics. Include a named quote if possible. Link to a full case study page. Place this section directly below the hero, above the product feature description.

new_sections_to_add.rationale: Factory, Windsurf, and Anthropic all use implicit or explicit contrast against 'autocomplete tools' or 'GUI-first AI'. Zerg's 'we do not stop at prompt-to-code' is the right instinct but it is a single sentence in body copy — it needs to be a section. Every enterprise buyer has Cursor/Copilot already deployed; they need to understand why Zerg is additive, not redundant.
new_sections_to_add.content_guidance: Two-column or three-row table: left column 'AI coding assistants', right column 'Zerg AI'. Rows: [Trigger] On demand → Continuous. [Validation] Unit tests and mocks → Live signals and real environments. [Delivery] Code generated → Software operating. [Drift handling] Manual re-prompt → Autonomous follow-on fixes. Keep it factual and non-disparaging — frame it as 'different job to be done', not 'we are better'. Title: 'Built for systems that need more than code generation.'

new_sections_to_add.rationale: Zerg's target market spans hardware startups (IoT control planes), financial infrastructure (migrations, CVE remediation), platform teams (data pipelines, vendor integrations), and enterprise IT (legacy modernization). No single buyer reads the subheadline list and thinks 'that's me'. Bolt's role-based cards (PM, Entrepreneur, Marketer) and Devin's use case descriptions (COBOL modernization, Nubank migration) both convert better than generic product descriptions because they trigger self-identification.
new_sections_to_add.content_guidance: 6 cards with icon, 4-word label, and 1-sentence description: [Hardware Integrations] → 'Connects AI-generated code to real device signals for validation'. [Legacy Migrations] → 'Migrates large codebases with reviewable checkpoints and real-environment testing'. [Vendor API Platforms] → 'Ships and maintains third-party integrations at 2-3x human team cadence'. [CVE Remediation] → 'Identifies, patches, and validates security fixes across production codebases'. [Data Pipeline Operations] → 'Builds and monitors ETL/ELT systems against live data sources'. [Production Control Systems] → 'Generates and operates software where hardware signals define correctness'. Each card links to a dedicated use case landing page or section.

new_sections_to_add.section: How Zerg works — visual Map→Build→Prove→Run flow
new_sections_to_add.rationale: Factory's tabbed 01-05 product flow, Replit's three-step visual onboarding, and Devin's metric-anchored feature blocks all outperform prose feature lists for product comprehension. Zerg's four-phase loop is the core intellectual differentiator but is currently only communicated in text bullets. A visual treatment makes it memorable (buyers should leave able to recite the four phases) and surfaces Prove and Run — the most differentiated phases — with appropriate visual weight.
new_sections_to_add.content_guidance: Horizontal four-step flow with connecting arrows: [1 Map] icon: grid/network → 'Models your codebase, APIs, hardware, and production rules' [2 Build] icon: code bracket → 'Generates integrations, migrations, and operational software to spec' [3 Prove] icon: checkmark/signal → 'Validates against live services, simulators, and real hardware signals — not mocks' [4 Run] icon: refresh/loop → 'Monitors for drift and ships autonomous follow-on fixes 24/7'. Use accent color (cream/beige on dark) for Prove and Run to signal they are the differentiating phases. Add a one-line below the flow: 'Most tools stop at step 2. Zerg doesn't.'

new_sections_to_add.rationale: Sourcegraph (SOC2 + ISO27001), GitHub Copilot (Trust Center + IP indemnity), Replit (SOC2 + SSO/SAML), and Windsurf (enterprise security) all surface compliance signals. Zerg targets mission-critical systems — the exact buyer segment with the most rigorous security procurement requirements. The complete absence of compliance signals is a conversion-stopper for enterprise security reviews.
new_sections_to_add.content_guidance: Minimal footer-adjacent band with 4 badges/labels: [SOC 2 Type II] (or 'Audit in progress' if not yet certified) | [Zero data retention — code never stored or shared] | [Private cloud deployment available] | [Enterprise SSO + RBAC]. Link to a /security page. Optional: add 'Enterprise terms and DPA available' for procurement teams. Tone: factual, not marketing-heavy — engineers and security reviewers are reading this, not buyers.
